questions.question: Define biomedical waste and list any four categories under BMW Rules, 2016.
keyPoints: Definition: Waste generated during diagnosis, treatment, immunization of humans/animals or in research activities
Categories: Yellow (anatomical, soiled, medicines)
Red (contaminated plastic items)
White (sharps)
Blue (glassware, metallic items)

questions.question: Explain the characteristics of biomedical waste that make it hazardous.
keyPoints: Infectious: contains pathogenic microorganisms
Sharps: can cause puncture injuries and transmit bloodborne diseases
Toxic/Chemical: contains harmful chemicals like formaldehyde, mercury
Pharmaceutical: expired drugs causing antibiotic resistance
Pathological: human/animal tissues with infection potential
Radioactive: from nuclear medicine procedures

questions.question: Describe the color coding system for biomedical waste segregation in India.
keyPoints: Yellow: Human anatomical waste, soiled waste, discarded medicines
Red: Contaminated plastic waste (gloves, tubing, bottles)
White: Sharps (needles, blades, broken glass)
Blue: Glassware and metallic implants

questions.question: What are the health hazards associated with improper biomedical waste management?
keyPoints: Needlestick injuries leading to HIV, Hepatitis B/C transmission
Airborne infections from contaminated dust
Chemical burns and poisonings
Environmental contamination
Antibiotic resistance from pharmaceutical waste
Psychological impact on waste handlers

questions.question: List any four treatment methods for biomedical waste and state one advantage of each.
keyPoints: Incineration: Complete destruction of pathological and pharmaceutical waste
Autoclaving: Steam sterilization is eco-friendly, no toxic emissions
Microwaving: Rapid treatment, effective for infectious waste
Chemical treatment: Suitable for liquid waste, quick disinfection
Deep burial: Simple method for remote areas, low cost

questions.question: What are the provisions regarding storage and transportation of biomedical waste under BMW Rules?
keyPoints: Storage: Maximum 48 hours in summer, 72 hours in winter
Storage area must be secured, covered, and leak-proof
Transportation: Use covered, leak-proof vehicles
Proper labeling with waste category and date
Dedicated bins for different waste types
Records maintained for waste movement

questions.question: Explain in detail the categories of biomedical waste as per Schedule I of BMW Rules, 2016. Give examples for each category.
keyPoints: Category 1 (Yellow): Human anatomical waste - body parts, tissues; Soiled waste - blood, bandages, gloves; Discarded medicines - expired drugs; Cytotoxic drugs - chemotherapy; Category 2 (Red): Contaminated plastic - tubing, gloves, masks, bottles; Category 3 (White): Sharps - needles, blades, broken glass; Category 4 (Blue): Glassware, metallic implants; Category 5: Liquid waste from labs; Category 6: Microbiology waste - cultures; Each with 1-2 examples

questions.question: A hospital generates 100 kg of biomedical waste daily. Explain the step-by-step procedure for its management from generation to final disposal, with reference to relevant provisions of BMW Rules, 2016.
keyPoints: Step 1: Segregation at source - color-coded containers
Step 2: Collection and storage - max 48/72 hours
Step 3: Transportation - authorized vehicles, proper labeling
Step 4: Treatment - based on category (incineration for yellow, autoclaving for red)
Step 5: Disposal - treated waste to secure landfill
Step 6: Documentation - maintain records for 5 years
Step 7: Authorization from SPCB mandatory
Step 8: Training and immunizations for handlers

questions.question: Discuss the various hazards associated with biomedical waste and suggest measures to minimize risks to healthcare workers and the environment.
keyPoints: Hazards: Infection risk (HIV, Hepatitis), injuries from sharps, toxic chemical exposure, air pollution from incineration, water contamination, mercury poisoning

Measures: Proper segregation, use of safety devices, PPE for workers, training programs, regular health checkups, proper waste treatment before disposal, use of alternative technologies, monitoring and compliance

questions.question: Compare the different treatment methods for biomedical waste (incineration, autoclaving, microwave, chemical) based on applicability, advantages, and limitations.
keyPoints: Incineration: Best for anatomical/cytotoxic, high cost, air pollution concerns
Autoclaving: For infectious waste, eco-friendly, not for pathological
Microwave: For solid/liquid waste, quick, requires pre-treatment
Chemical: For liquid waste, effective, handle chemicals carefully
Comparison table format acceptable
Selection based on waste category and facility resources
